title: What Your Cafe Stands For: Defining Values That People Can Feel
subtitle: Values are not a poster on the wall. They are how people behave when the owner is not watching.

toc.label: The Staff Handbook That Is Not a Threat

sections.heading: Why Values Are Not Soft
sections.body:
In small businesses, values get dismissed as corporate fluff. "We don't need values — we just need to make good coffee and be nice to people."

But "be nice" is not a standard. What does nice look like when a customer is rude? When a team member consistently arrives late? When someone's idea of clean is different from yours?

Values are decision-making shortcuts. When the team knows the cafe values craft, they calibrate the grinder without being told. When they know the cafe values warmth, they greet every customer without a policy requiring it. When they know the cafe values accountability, they own their mistakes instead of hiding them.

Without articulated values, the cafe defaults to the owner's personality. That works when the owner is present. It fails when they are not — because nobody else knows the unspoken rules.

Values also drive hiring. If you can articulate what you stand for, you can hire people who share those beliefs. If you cannot, you hire for skill alone and hope for cultural fit. Sometimes you get it. Often you do not, and you spend six months frustrated that someone who makes great coffee does not care about the things you care about.

sections.heading: The Values Discovery Process
sections.body:
Values are not invented. They are discovered — they already exist in how you run the cafe on your best days.

Use T172-01 (Values Discovery Worksheet):

**Step 1: Reflect on peak moments.** Think about the best days in the cafe — the shifts that felt right, the interactions that made you proud, the moments you would show someone if they asked what the cafe is about. Write down what made those moments special.

**Step 2: Identify deal-breakers.** What behaviour would you never tolerate, even from your most skilled team member? Rudeness to customers? Cutting corners on food safety? Gossiping about colleagues? These reveal your values in negative space.

**Step 3: Ask the team.** Use T175-01 (Culture Baseline Interview Guide). Individually ask each team member: "What do you think this cafe stands for?" and "What makes a good day here?" You will hear themes — those themes are your values.

**Step 4: Distil to 3–5 words or phrases.** Not sentences — concepts. Examples:
- **Craft** — we care about quality in everything we make
- **Warmth** — every person who walks in should feel welcome
- **Honesty** — we say what we mean and own our mistakes
- **Growth** — we help each other get better
- **Community** — we are part of this neighbourhood, not just in it

**Step 5: Test them.** Read each value and ask: "Would I fire someone who consistently violated this, even if they were good at their job?" If yes, it is a real value. If no, it is an aspiration — nice to have but not core.

Do not copy another cafe's values. Your values must be authentically yours or the team will see through them immediately.

sections.heading: From Values to Standards
sections.body:
Values without behaviours are slogans. The bridge between "we value warmth" and a warm cafe is specific, observable standards.

For each value, define 2–3 behaviours that demonstrate it:

**Craft:**
- We calibrate the grinder before every shift and taste the first shot
- We follow recipe cards for every food item
- We never serve something we are not proud of

**Warmth:**
- We greet every customer within 10 seconds of them entering
- We use the customer's name if we know it
- We make eye contact and put the phone down when someone approaches

**Honesty:**
- If we make a mistake, we say so immediately — to the customer and to the team
- We give feedback directly and kindly, not behind someone's back
- We share financial information with the team so everyone understands the business

**Growth:**
- Every team member has a development goal and we check in on it monthly
- We celebrate when someone learns a new skill
- We invest time in training, even when we are busy

These standards are what go in the staff handbook, what get covered in onboarding, what get reinforced in team meetings, and what get used in performance conversations.

The key word is observable. "Be passionate about coffee" is not observable. "Taste the espresso at every calibration and adjust if it's not right" is.

sections.heading: The Staff Handbook That Is Not a Threat
sections.body:
Most staff handbooks read like legal threats. "Failure to comply will result in disciplinary action." Nobody reads them, nobody refers to them, and they sit in a drawer until someone is being fired.

A good staff handbook (T173-01) is a welcome document that answers every question a team member might have, in a tone that reflects your values.

**What it should include:**
- Our story — why the cafe exists and what we care about
- Our values — with the specific behaviours defined above
- Practical information — pay dates, holiday process, shift swaps, who to call when sick
- Standards — dress code, phone policy, timekeeping expectations
- Policies — allergens, food safety, complaints, social media
- Development — how progression works, what training is available
- Support — wellbeing resources, who to talk to if something is wrong

**What it should NOT be:**
- A legal document (keep HR compliance separate or in an appendix)
- A list of rules with no context
- Longer than 15–20 pages
- Written in formal corporate language

**Tone matters.** Write it like you talk. If your cafe is warm and informal, the handbook should be too. "We trust you to manage your time. If you're running late, text the shift lead — no drama, just communication" lands better than "Persistent lateness will be managed through the company disciplinary procedure."

Give it to every new hire on day one. Refer to it in training. Update it annually. Make it a living document, not a legal artefact.

sections.heading: Sharing the Owner's Vision
sections.body:
Every cafe owner has a picture in their head of what the cafe could be. The problem is that this picture is rarely shared — it lives in the owner's mind, shaping their decisions and frustrations, invisible to the team.

The result: the team does not understand why the owner cares so much about the details. The owner feels alone in caring. A gap grows between what the owner wants and what the team delivers.

Use T176-01 (Owner Vision Discovery Worksheet) to articulate your vision, then share it.

**The vision conversation with the team:**

This is not a corporate presentation. It is a genuine conversation — ideally over coffee, during a team meeting or an informal get-together.

"I want to tell you what I see for this place. Not just what it is today, but what I want it to become."

Share:
- **Where you started** — why you opened the cafe, what drove you
- **Where you are** — what is working, what you are proud of
- **Where you are going** — what the cafe looks like in 2–3 years. A second site? A community hub? The best specialty cafe in the area? A lifestyle business that gives you balance?
- **What you need from them** — not just work, but partnership. "I need people who care about this as much as I do."

This conversation changes dynamics. Team members who understand the vision start making decisions aligned with it — without being told. They take ownership because they feel like part of something, not just employees doing shifts.

Revisit the vision annually. It evolves — and the team should evolve with it.

sections.heading: When Culture Goes Wrong
sections.body:
Toxic dynamics in a small team are devastating. In a cafe with 5–8 people, one person with a bad attitude can poison the entire environment.

Use T174-01 (Toxic Behaviour Definition Guide) to name what toxic looks like, so everyone recognises it:

**Gossip.** Talking about a colleague behind their back instead of to their face. "Did you see what she did?" is not venting — it is corrosion.

**Passive resistance.** Agreeing to a standard in the meeting and ignoring it on the shift. "Yeah, sure" followed by no change.

**Cliques.** Two or three people who exclude others, create an in-group/out-group dynamic, and make new hires feel unwelcome.

**Undermining.** Correcting or contradicting a colleague in front of customers. Rolling eyes at a manager's instructions. Sabotaging someone else's work by not doing their share.

**How to intervene:**

**1. Name it early.** The longer toxic behaviour continues, the harder it is to address. A private, direct conversation within 48 hours: "I've noticed [specific behaviour]. It doesn't align with how we work here. What's going on?"

**2. Listen.** Sometimes toxic behaviour is a symptom. Burnout, personal problems, feeling undervalued — the behaviour is the surface, not the root. Listen before judging.

**3. Set clear expectations.** "Going forward, I need [specific behaviour change]. I'm here to support you, but this is non-negotiable."

**4. Follow through.** If the behaviour continues after a direct conversation, it is a choice. And choices have consequences. A team member who makes the team worse is not worth keeping, regardless of their skill.

Protecting culture means being willing to lose a good barista to keep a good team. That is one of the hardest decisions an owner makes — and one of the most important.
